seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

print("Current M squares:")

        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: boundary={n['boundary']}")

    prev_nodes = prev_seg['nodes']
    print("\nPrevious M squares:")
    for n in prev_nodes:
        if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 4:
            print(f"  Node {n['id']}: boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! Node 9 moved from [34-35, 38-39] to [33-34, 37-38]. That's up-left by 1! So clicking on the top-left corner of Node 9 just moved it up-left by 1, not a swap.
But earlier, clicking on the top-left corner of Node 10 at [35, 39] caused a swap with Node 9. Let me reconsider...
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the previous click. In transition 48, I clicked at [35, 39], and Node 10 was at [35-36, 39-40]. So [35, 39] is the top-left corner of Node 10. And Node 9 was at [35-36, 16-17].
After the click:
- Node 10 moved to [35-36, 16-17] (Node 9's old position)
- Node 9 moved to [34-35, 38-39] (Node 10's old position, up-left by 1)
So it seems like clicking on the top-left corner of Node 10 caused it to swap with Node 9. But clicking on the top-left corner of Node 9 just moved it up-left by 1.
The difference is that Node 10 was to the right of Node 9, and clicking on Node 10's top-left corner caused it to swap with Node 9 (which is to its left). But Node 9 was already to the left, and clicking on its top-left corner just moved it up-left by 1.
Maybe the mechanic is: clicking on a magenta square moves it up-left by 1. If the new position overlaps with another magenta square, they swap.
Let me check: Node 10 was at [35-36, 39-40], and moving up-left by 1 would put it at [34-35, 38-39]. But Node 9 was at [35-36, 16-17], so there's no overlap. So this doesn't explain the swap.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. Maybe the click position relative to the magenta square matters:
- Clicking on the center of a magenta square moves it up-left by 1.
- Clicking on the top-left corner of a magenta square causes it to move to the position of the magenta square that's closest in the top-left direction.
In transition 48, I clicked at [35, 39], which is the top-left corner of Node 10. Node 9 was at [35-36, 16-17], which is to the left of Node 10. So clicking on the top-left corner of Node 10 caused it to move to Node 9's position.
And Node 9 moved to [34-35, 38-39], which is Node 10's old position shifted up-left by 1.
So the mechanic might be: clicking on the top-left corner of a magenta square causes it to swap with the magenta square that's closest in the top-left direction, and the other square moves up-left by 1.
But in this latest click, I clicked at [34, 38], which is the top-left corner of Node 9. Node 9 moved from [34-35, 38-39] to [33-34, 37-38], which is up-left by 1. No swap occurred.
Maybe the swap only happens if there's another magenta square in the top-left direction. Node 9 is at [34-35, 38-39], and the closest magenta square in the top-left direction would be... Node 10 is at [35-36, 16-17], which is to the left but not above. Node 12 is at [55-56, 14-15], which is below and to the left. So there's no magenta square in the top-left direction of Node 9, so clicking on its top-left corner just moves it up-left by 1.
OK so let me reconsider the mechanic:
- Clicking on a magenta square moves it up-left by 1.
- If there's another magenta square in the direction of the click (relative to the clicked square), they swap.
Actually, I think I'm overcomplicating this. Let me just focus on the goal. The magenta squares are moving up-left when clicked. The blue shape is at [23-31, 29-37]. Maybe the goal is to move all magenta squares into the blue shape.
Current positions:
- Node 9: [33-34, 37-38] (moved up-left by 1 from [34-35, 38-39])
- Node 10: [35-36, 16-17]
- Node 11: [54-55, 45-46]
- Node 12: [55-56, 14-15]
Node 9 is getting closer to the blue shape. Let me continue clicking on Node 9 to move it into the blue shape.
But wait, I've used 32 actions out of 42 target actions. I only have 10 actions left. If I need to move each magenta square into the blue shape, that would take many more actions.
Let me reconsider the goal. Maybe the goal is not to move the magenta squares into the blue shape. Maybe the goal is something else.
Actually, let me look at the blue shape more carefully. It's at [23-31, 29-37], and it has 69 pixels. The boundary is [[23, 31], [23, 35], [25, 37], [29, 37], [31, 35], [31, 31], [29, 29], [25, 29]]. This is an octagonal shape.
Maybe the blue shape is a "portal" or "target" that the magenta squares need to reach. When a magenta square reaches the blue shape, it might disappear or change color.
Let me try clicking on Node 9 a few more times to move it closer to the blue shape, and see if anything special happens when it reaches the blue shape.
Actually, Node 9 is at [33-34, 37-38], and the blue shape is at [23-31, 29-37]. Node 9's column range [37-38] overlaps with the blue shape's column range [29-37] at column 37. And Node 9's row range [33-34] is below the blue shape's row range [23-31]. So Node 9 needs to move up by about 2 rows to reach the blue shape.
Let me click on Node 9 twice more to move it into the blue shape.
